questions.question: Define water-borne diseases and give two examples.
keyPoints: Water-borne diseases are caused by pathogens transmitted through contaminated water.
Examples: Cholera, Typhoid, Dysentery, Hepatitis A, Giardiasis

questions.question: Explain the fecal-oral transmission route with an example.
keyPoints: Pathogens in feces enter mouth through contaminated water/food.
Example: Hepatitis A virus in stool contaminates drinking water; healthy person drinks it and gets infected.
Poor hygiene and sanitation facilitate this route.

questions.question: List three preventive measures for water-borne diseases.
keyPoints: Boiling or filtering drinking water
Proper sewage disposal and sanitation
Hand washing with soap after toilet use
Using ORS for diarrhea treatment
Avoiding swimming in contaminated water

questions.question: Differentiate between water-borne and water-induced diseases.
keyPoints: Water-borne: pathogens directly in water, ingested (e.g., cholera from contaminated water)
Water-induced: vectors breed in water, disease transmitted by bite (e.g., malaria from mosquito breeding in water)
Water-borne = drinking contaminated water; Water-induced = water creates vector breeding sites

questions.question: What is ORS? Explain its importance in treating water-borne diarrheal diseases.
keyPoints: Oral Rehydration Solution - mixture of salt, sugar, and clean water.
Replaces lost fluids and electrolytes during severe diarrhea
Prevents death from dehydration in cholera and other diarrheal diseases
Simple, cost-effective treatment usable at home

questions.question: Name the causative organisms for: (a) Cholera (b) Typhoid (c) Giardiasis
keyPoints: (a) Vibrio cholerae (b) Salmonella typhi (c) Giardia lamblia
All are different types: bacterial, bacterial, and protozoan parasite respectively

questions.question: Explain the relationship between poor sanitation and water-borne diseases.
keyPoints: Poor sanitation leads to human waste contaminating water sources
Contaminated water becomes vehicle for pathogens
Lack of proper sewage disposal increases fecal-oral transmission
Areas with poor sanitation have higher incidence of cholera, typhoid, hepatitis A

questions.question: Describe the symptoms, causative organism, and prevention of Cholera.
keyPoints: Symptoms: Rice-water diarrhea, vomiting, severe dehydration, abdominal cramps, rapid fluid loss
Causative organism: Vibrio cholerae (serogroups O1 and O139) - gram-negative bacterium producing cholera toxin
Prevention: Boiling/filtering water, proper sanitation, hand washing, avoiding raw/undercooked seafood, ORS for treatment, sewage disposal

questions.question: Discuss the life cycle of malaria parasite and its connection to water.
keyPoints: Female Anopheles mosquito breeds in clean stagnant water (ponds, puddles)
Mosquito bites infected person, takes gametocytes
Parasite develops in mosquito salivary glands
Mosquito bites healthy person, sporozoites enter bloodstream
In liver, sporozoites multiply, release merozoites
Merozoites infect red blood cells causing malaria symptoms
Water → mosquito breeding → malaria transmission cycle

questions.question: Explain Hepatitis A: transmission, symptoms, and preventive measures.
keyPoints: Transmission: Fecal-oral route via contaminated water/food, close contact with infected person
Symptoms: Fever, fatigue, loss of appetite, nausea, abdominal pain, jaundice, dark urine - 2-6 week incubation
Prevention: Hepatitis A vaccine, boiling drinking water, proper sanitation, hand washing after toilet
No specific treatment - supportive care, most recover completely

questions.question: Describe the vector mosquitoes responsible for water-induced diseases in India with their breeding sites and diseases transmitted.
keyPoints: Anopheles: Clean stagnant freshwater - ponds, rice fields; transmits Malaria
Aedes aegypti: Stagnant water in containers, tires; transmits Dengue, Chikungunya, Zika
Culex: Dirty/ polluted water, drains, sewage; transmits Filariasis, Japanese Encephalitis
Mansonia: Water with aquatic vegetation; transmits Filariasis
Prevention: Eliminate breeding sites, use mosquito nets, repellents, larvicides
